blocks.text: Chapter 7.
Testing and Finding Hook Opportunities
The ideas in 30 seconds
The Hook Model is testable in the real world, and the Bible App shows it works far outside consumer tech's usual haunts.
Habit Testing runs in three steps: Identify, Codify, and Modify.
Identify uses cohort data to define who your devoted users are and how often a committed user should engage.
Codify finds the habit path — the shared sequence of actions your most loyal users take.
Modify changes the product to steer new users down that path, then tests again in a continuous loop.
Fresh opportunities cluster in three places: nascent behaviors, enabling technologies, and interface changes.
The Bible App Proves the Model Travels

Eyal grounds the closing chapter in a case that deliberately isn't a Silicon Valley darling: the Bible App, or YouVersion, built by Bobby Gruenewald. Starting from a simple idea, it grew into a habit for tens of millions of people who open it daily. It runs the full Hook Model. External and owned triggers arrive as daily-verse notifications and reminders. The action is opening the app to read a short, easily digestible passage. Variable rewards come through the range of content, plans, and social features that make each visit a little different. And users invest by highlighting verses, saving notes, building reading plans, and following along — depositing stored value that personalizes the app and loads future triggers.

The point of choosing this example is to show the model isn't a trick specific to games or social feeds. A product serving a devout, everyday purpose, built by a team outside the usual tech centers, formed durable daily habits by systematically applying the same four steps. If the Hook Model can build a reading habit around scripture, its logic generalizes — which is exactly why Eyal wants builders to test for habits rigorously rather than assume them.
Habit Testing: Identify, Codify, Modify

Metrics can flatter you. A product can show growth and activity while forming no real habit at all — curiosity and one-time novelty look a lot like engagement in a dashboard. To separate genuine habits from wishful numbers, Eyal prescribes Habit Testing, a three-step process that runs continuously.

Identify. First, determine who your habitual users actually are. This requires cohort analysis — grouping users by when they joined and watching how their behavior holds up over time. Before you look, form a hypothesis about what a devoted user looks like: how often should someone use the product if they've formed a habit? A social app might expect several visits a day; a different product, once a week. Defining that threshold in advance keeps you honest, and the cohort data then tells you how many users clear it. Those are your habitual users — the population worth studying.

Codify. With habitual users identified, dig into what they do that distinguishes them, hunting for the habit path — the series of similar actions that your most engaged users share on their way to becoming devoted. The classic illustration of this kind of discovery is Twitter's early finding that new users who followed a certain number of accounts — around thirty — were far more likely to stick, revealing a specific behavioral milestone that predicted retention. The habit path is that shared route: the actions loyal users almost all take, which new users who churn tend to skip.

Modify. Once you've codified the path, change the product to guide new users down it. Redesign onboarding, prompts, and defaults to steer newcomers toward the behaviors your devoted users share — then run the test again. Habit Testing isn't a one-time audit; it's an ongoing cycle of measuring, finding the path, nudging users toward it, and re-measuring as the product and its users evolve. Each turn sharpens your understanding of what actually creates a habit in your specific product.
Where New Habits Become Possible

Beyond testing an existing product, Eyal offers guidance on where to find opportunities to build new habit-forming products — moments when user behavior is fluid enough that a new habit can take root. He points to three fertile places.

Nascent behaviors are things few people do yet — emerging behaviors on the fringe that could go mainstream. Habits are hardest to form when a behavior is already locked in around an incumbent, and easiest when the behavior itself is new and unclaimed. Early photo-sharing was a nascent behavior before it became universal; the builders who noticed people beginning to share images from phones, before it was obvious, had room to install the habit. Watching what enthusiasts and early adopters are starting to do — behaviors that seem marginal now — is a way to spot the habits of the future before they're contested.

Enabling technologies are new technical capabilities that make the hook cycle faster, easier, or more rewarding than before. When a technology removes friction from the action, tightens the loop, or intensifies the reward, behaviors that were previously too cumbersome to become habits suddenly can. A leap in speed, a new sensor, a cheaper connection — each can turn a rare behavior into a frequent one by lowering the cost of running the loop. Builders who ride a new enabling technology can form habits that simply weren't possible a moment earlier.

Interface changes are shifts in the way people interact with technology, and they repeatedly throw habits up for grabs. When the interface changes — a new input method, a new device, a new way of transacting — the old habits built for the old interface no longer fit, and there's a scramble to establish the new default. Each major interface shift redistributes which companies own users' reflexes, because habits tuned to the previous way of doing things have to be reformed. The builders who move first on a new interface can capture habits that were, moments before, locked up by incumbents.

Across all three, the common thread is fluidity: habits form most readily where behavior isn't yet settled. Find a nascent behavior, a new enabling technology, or a fresh interface, apply the Hook Model deliberately, and test for real habits with Identify-Codify-Modify — that, in sum, is Eyal's method for building products people can't put down, and for understanding the ones that already have that grip on you.
In practice
Define in advance how often a genuinely habitual user should engage, then use cohort analysis to see how many users actually clear that bar.
Study your most devoted users to codify the habit path — the shared sequence of actions that separates them from users who churn.
Redesign onboarding and defaults to steer new users down that path, then re-run Habit Testing as a continuous loop, not a one-time check.
Scout for opportunities in nascent behaviors, enabling technologies, and interface changes — habits form most easily where behavior isn't yet settled.
Don't mistake growth or activity metrics for a habit; only cohort retention against a pre-set frequency threshold proves one exists.
As a user, ask which of your own daily reflexes were installed by a product deliberately steering you down its habit path — and which you'd keep if you chose consciously.
